title: Best practices for state recovery after VPS/container restarts in live spot execution systems?
author: Severe_Waltz_1371
selftext:
I’m working on a Binance spot execution engine (VPS + Docker) and I’m trying to make restart/recovery safe.



After a VPS/container restart, what do you consider best practice:

1) rebuild state purely from exchange open orders and balances, or

2) persist local state and reconcile it with the exchange on startup?



Also, what are your “must-have” invariants to detect inconsistent order sets (TP/SL/trailing) without spamming alerts?

comments.author: Patient-Bumblebee
comments.body:
There will almost always be some state that you cant get directly from the exchange so makes sense to have a local DB imo. 

But yes, definitely sync it with the exchange and have checks in place.

replies.author: Severe_Waltz_1371
replies.body:
Yeah, that matches my experience as well.

There’s always some execution-related state you can’t reliably reconstruct from the exchange alone

(e.g. intent, partial fills history, trailing logic, internal guards),

so persisting local state makes sense.

On restart I’ve been leaning toward:

– restoring local state

– then reconciling it against live exchange orders and balances

– and flagging any divergence via invariants instead of blindly trusting either side



Curious if you’ve seen specific failure modes where relying too much on local state caused issues.

comments.author: user_alpha231
comments.body: Curious how you’re handling partial fills + orphan TP/SL after restart , that’s usually where things get tricky

replies.author: Severe_Waltz_1371
replies.body:
I’ve learned that treating a restart as a \*reconciliation phase\*, not a resume, works best.



On startup I currently lean toward:

– restoring local execution intent/state (filled qty, TP/SL structure, trailing logic)

– pulling live open orders and recent fills from the exchange

– rebuilding the effective position from actual fills

– and validating everything via invariants instead of blindly trusting either side



Partial fills and orphaned TP/SL after restarts are the hardest cases so far.

If invariants detect a broken set, I avoid silent auto-fixes:

either cancel and rebuild a consistent order set, or stop execution and alert, depending on severity.



Curious how others decide where to draw the line between auto-repair and manual intervention.

replies.author: user_alpha231
replies.body: Out of curiosity, do you ever auto-repair orphaned TP/SL or always force manual intervention? That line is hard to draw in production.

replies.author: Severe_Waltz_1371
replies.body:
I don’t treat it as always auto-repair vs always manual — it’s severity-based.



For simple, unambiguous cases (e.g. a missing protective SL after restart or a known SL transition),

I allow limited auto-repair like cancel + rebuild, or restoring protection.



But if the state is ambiguous (partial fills, qty mismatch, conflicting orders),

I stop execution and alert instead of doing silent fixes.



In production I’d rather halt than guess — auto-repair is only acceptable when it’s clearly safe.
